skill.name: agent-creator
skill.description: Creates specialized AI agents with optimized system prompts using the official 4-phase SOP methodology from Desktop .claude-flow, combined with evidence-based prompting techniques and Claude Agent SDK implementation. Use this skill when creating production-ready agents for specific domains, workflows, or tasks requiring consistent high-quality performance with deeply embedded domain knowledge.
skill.summary: Create specialized AI agents with structured prompts, domain research, SDK examples, and validation workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: The weak-crypto, SAM database, and reconnaissance detections are false positives caused by markdown text, diagram labels, and the phrase SYSTEM PROMPT. The skill is documentation-only, but copied SDK examples grant agents Bash access, start an npx MCP server, and show permissive permission modes, so publication should include a command-execution warning.

medium_findings.title: SDK Examples Grant Shell-Capable Tools
medium_findings.description: The TypeScript and Python SDK examples include Bash in the allowed tool list and start an MCP server through npx. This is not executed by the skill itself, but users who copy the examples could give generated agents command execution ability without enough guardrails.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-capable configuration is explicit in the SDK examples. Risk depends on users copying the example into a real implementation, so the confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Permissive Permission Mode Is Documented Without Strong Warning
medium_findings.description: The process diagram lists bypassPermissions as a mode that auto-approves all actions. The file is a diagram, not executable code, but presenting this mode in the main creation flow may lead users to unsafe agent configurations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permission mode text is directly present, but the diagram does not instruct users to enable it in code. The concern is contextual misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches Are Mostly Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, Windows SAM database, and reconnaissance findings do not correspond to executable cryptography, credential access, or scanning logic. The flagged content is markdown prose, template text, code fences, and Graphviz labels.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows these locations are documentation or diagram nodes, not executable code. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, SAM access, or network reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Pasteable Agent Configuration With Bash Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The SDK snippets show agents configured with Bash and external MCP startup commands. This pattern needs explicit user review before production use because it expands an agent's ability to affect the local system.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippets contain concrete tool and command settings. They are examples rather than active scripts, which limits immediate risk.

content.value_statement: Teams often create agents with vague roles, weak guardrails, and inconsistent outputs. This skill provides a structured method for designing domain-specific agents, prompts, workflows, and validation checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through a four-phase agent creation workflow.
Provides templates for role definition, expertise modeling, and guardrails.
Shows how to document MCP integration and agent coordination patterns.
Includes TypeScript and Python Claude Agent SDK example structures.
Defines validation gates for prompts, workflows, and failure modes.
Supports planning for analytical, generative, diagnostic, and orchestration agents.
limitations: It is a methodology document, not a finished agent generator.
SDK snippets require review before use in production environments.
It does not verify current SDK syntax or package versions.
Generated agents still need domain expert testing and security review.

use_cases.title: Create a Domain Support Agent
use_cases.description: Design an agent with clear scope, domain knowledge, escalation rules, and quality checks.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal Agent Design
use_cases.description: Use one workflow for agent specifications, prompt structure, tool access, and validation gates.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Production Agent Package
use_cases.description: Plan SDK implementation, MCP coordination, test cases, documentation, and maintenance practices.

prompt_templates.title: Define a Simple Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-creator to define a specialized agent for [task]. Include role, scope, required knowledge, common failure modes, and success criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Prompt Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-creator to turn this specification into a system prompt: [agent specification]. Add identity, workflow, guardrails, validation checks, and output requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Agent Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-creator to design a workflow where [agent A] coordinates with [agent B]. Define handoffs, memory keys, escalation points, and validation gates.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare SDK Implementation Guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-creator to prepare production guidance for [agent name]. Include SDK structure, tool permissions, MCP server assumptions, tests, rollout risks, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Create a customer research agent for a product team.
output: Agent role and scope focused on customer insight synthesis.
Domain knowledge map covering interviews, feedback channels, and segmentation.
Guardrails for evidence quality, privacy boundaries, and unsupported claims.
Validation checklist for repeatable research summaries.

output_examples.input: Design a diagnostic agent for deployment failures.
output: Troubleshooting workflow with evidence collection before action.
Failure mode handbook covering configuration, dependency, and rollout issues.
Escalation rules for destructive commands or unclear production impact.
Test plan for common and edge-case deployment incidents.
System prompt outline with identity, tools, workflows, and success criteria.
Permission review notes for file access, shell access, and external services.
Documentation plan with usage examples, known limits, and maintenance steps.

best_practices: Start with domain analysis before writing the system prompt.
Define tool permissions and approval modes before implementation.
Test generated agents with typical cases, edge cases, and failure cases.
anti_patterns: Do not grant Bash or write access without a clear operational need.
Do not skip failure mode documentation for production agents.
Do not treat template examples as production code without review.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create specialized AI agents with structured prompts, domain knowledge, workflows, guardrails, and validation plans.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete working agent automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a methodology and examples that users adapt into a finished implementation.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is compatible with Claude, Claude Code, and Codex workflows for agent planning.

faq.question: Does it require MCP servers?
faq.answer: No. MCP integration is optional, but the skill includes patterns for agents that coordinate through MCP tools.

faq.question: What security review is needed?
faq.answer: Review tool permissions, shell access, external commands, and approval modes before using SDK examples.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is useful for AI platform teams, automation engineers, and developers who design repeatable agent workflows.
